Name: Expense Approval
Description: Employees submit line-item reports; totals, over-budget, and escalation flags cascade automatically.

Employees.Description: Table: Employees — people who submit expense reports or review them.
Employees.summary_rich: The **cast** of the approval workflow. Each employee plays one of three roles — *employee* (submitter, has a `BudgetLimit`), *manager* (reviews escalated reports), or *finance* (final sign-off). The per-submitter `BudgetLimit` is the threshold every report's total is measured against; raise Dave from $200 to $400 and his pending Team Lunch stops tripping the over-budget gate immediately.

schema.name: BudgetLimit
schema.Description: Per-report ceiling above which reports are flagged over-budget. Applies to submitters; reviewers (manager/finance) can be left null.
schema.explanation_rich: **The dollar threshold that turns a routine report into an escalation.** Each submitter carries a per-report ceiling on their employee row — Alice can spend up to $500 per report, Dave up to $200. Managers and Finance carry `null` because they review, they don't submit. Worked example: Alice's *NYC Client Visit* totals $695 against her $500 limit, so `IsOverBudget` flips `TRUE` and the report lands in the manager's escalation queue. Raise her limit to $700 and the same report clears on the next read — no code change, no migration, just the DAG recomputing.

ExpenseReports.Description: Table: ExpenseReports — one submission, containing many line items.
ExpenseReports.summary_rich: The **forms filed in triplicate**. Each report belongs to one submitter and rolls up many line items. Three calculated fields wire the approval gates in a single cascade: `TotalAmount` sums the line items, `IsOverBudget` compares that total to the submitter's `BudgetLimit`, and `RequiresEscalation` flags any over-budget report that's still `pending`. Add one $50 line item to Dave's pending Team Lunch and watch the cascade re-fire — total goes from $87 to $137, still under his $200 cap, no escalation. Add a $200 line item instead and the same report jumps into the manager's escalation queue on the next read.

schema.name: TotalAmount
schema.Description: Sum of all line-item amounts on this report.

schema.name: IsOverBudget
schema.Description: True when TotalAmount exceeds the employee's BudgetLimit.
schema.explanation_rich: **The first gate.** Compares the report's rolled-up `TotalAmount` against the submitter's looked-up `BudgetLimit` — both fields are themselves derived (one aggregated via `SUMIFS`, one looked up via `INDEX/MATCH`), so this is a *third-order* calculation built on top of two prior tiers. The `AND(EmployeeBudgetLimit>0, …)` guard is deliberate: reviewers with `null` limits don't trip the gate when they file a personal expense. Worked example: Alice's *NYC Client Visit* — line items $425 + $185 + $85 = $695 against her $500 ceiling → `TRUE`. Edit the hotel from $185 down to $40 and the total drops to $550, still over → still `TRUE`. Drop it to $30 and the total drops to $540, still over. Only when the total dips below $500 does this field flip — and every substrate flips with it on the next read.

schema.name: RequiresEscalation
schema.Description: Over-budget AND not yet approved — manager's escalation queue.
schema.explanation_rich: **The second gate, and the one a manager actually sees on their morning queue.** A report enters the escalation queue only when *both* are true: it's over its submitter's budget (`IsOverBudget=TRUE`) **and** it isn't yet approved (`IsApproved=FALSE`). The conjunction matters — an over-budget report that finance has already approved doesn't keep nagging; a pending report under-budget doesn't, either. Worked example: Alice's *NYC Client Visit* is $695 over a $500 limit and `pending`, so this is `TRUE` and it shows up in Bob's escalation queue. Set its `ApprovalStatus` to `approved` and the gate flips `FALSE` on the next read — same rulebook, same DAG, no application code touched.

ExpenseItems.Description: Table: ExpenseItems — individual line items on a report.

schema.name: Amount
schema.Description: Dollar amount of this line item.

__meta__.Description: Project-level metadata that travels with the rulebook: tagline, motif, narrative descriptions, substrate list, signature rows, etc. One row per metadata key. Use ValueType to interpret StringValue vs JsonValue.

schema.name: MetaKey
schema.Description: The metadata key (e.g. 'tagline', 'motif_palette', 'substrates'). Unique within the table.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Identifier for this metadata entry. Mirrors MetaKey so the row is addressable by Name like every other table.

schema.name: ValueType
schema.Description: How to interpret the value columns: 'string' (use StringValue), 'object' (parse JsonValue as JSON object), 'array' (parse JsonValue as JSON array).

schema.name: StringValue
schema.Description: Plain string value. Populated when ValueType == 'string'; null otherwise.

schema.name: JsonValue
schema.Description: JSON-encoded value. Populated when ValueType == 'object' or 'array'; null when ValueType == 'string'.

data.Name: tagline
data.StringValue: Reports filed in triplicate — totals roll up, budgets gate, managers escalate.

data.Name: description_rich
data.StringValue: Expense Approval is the classic **threshold-and-escalation** demo: a stack of submitted reports, each with a stack of line items, each measured against the submitter's personal per-report ceiling. The interesting thing isn't the data, it's the **third-order cascade**: raw line-item amounts → `TotalAmount` (aggregation) → `IsOverBudget` (calculated, gated on the looked-up `EmployeeBudgetLimit`) → `RequiresEscalation` (calculated, gated on approval status). Edit one $42 line item and the cascade re-fires top to bottom in every substrate — Postgres view, Python module, OWL ontology — without a single line of application code mediating between them.

data.Name: use_cases
data.JsonValue: ["**Escalate an over-budget report.** Open *NYC Client Visit*, see Alice's $695 total tripping her $500 limit, watch `IsOverBudget` and `RequiresEscalation` both fire while `ApprovalStatus` is still `pending`.", "**Approve out of the escalation queue.** Set *NYC Client Visit*'s `ApprovalStatus` to `approved`. `IsApproved` flips, the `AND` in `RequiresEscalation` collapses, and the report leaves the manager's queue on the next read.", "**Raise a submitter's budget.** Change Dave's `BudgetLimit` from $200 to $500. Every report Dave has filed re-evaluates the over-budget gate — no migration, no app deploy, the rulebook recomputes the lookups.", "**Add a line item to a pending report.** Append a $200 expense to *Team Lunch* ($87 → $287); the aggregation re-fires, Dave's $200 ceiling is now exceeded, and a previously-quiet report enters the escalation queue.", "**Ask the same threshold question in two substrates.** *\"How many reports need escalation right now?\"* runs as a Postgres view, a Python comprehension, an OWL axiom — same number, every time."]

data.Name: journal_seed
data.StringValue: Three reports on the desk: one approved, one quiet, one escalating. Alice has overspent on NYC by $195; Bob's queue has exactly one form in it.
